title: Taking a Look at Mini Rogue, and Expansions - Tabletop Rogue Like
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=MM879hqJf1U

Daniel (@dungeondive) reviews Nuts Publishing's *Mini Rogue* — a small-box solo/2P roguelike where each run takes you through four floors (areas 2/2/3/3) and four bosses, ending in Og's Remains. Bosses for any given game are drawn at random from a pool, so you never know which three minor bosses you'll face before the final.

The engine is a single Hero die and Dungeon die rolled together, augmented by stat upgrades (more Hero dice in the pool) and feats (re-rolls at cost). The combat stance card — aggressive/balanced/defensive — is a smart optional rule that lets you tune crit and miss ranges. Exploding sixes paired with a one-in-six miss-on-X create the press-your-luck spine; you can build to twelve damage on two sixes and watch one bad die wipe the result.

Daniel's larger pitch: a roguelike board game with *Rogue Legacy*-style meta-progression is still missing from the genre, and *Mini Rogue*'s campaign skill system could probably be hacked into it. His main complaint is icon density without an in-box reference card — the BGG appendix is essentially required. Glittering Treasure (cosmetic), Depths of Damnation (story cards + competitive mode), and Potions & Perils are all rolled into this overview.

----

*Mini Rogue* is, by Daniel's own admission, mostly luck. Is that the right design for a small-box roguelike, or does the genre demand the strategy ceiling that something like *Unbroken* aims at?
